[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2025-01-24
[TITLE]: Gridlocked: Tackling India's Traffic Congestion Crisis
[METADATA/INTRO]: Gridlocked: Tackling India's Traffic Congestion Crisis India has only 1.2 buses per 1000 people which is far below the benchmarks for developing nations. Strengthening public transport should be a priority....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Grounding the Congestion Problem 
 As per the Road Transport Yearbook 2019-20, the number of registered motor vehicles per 1000 people has increased from 53 in 2001 to 246 in 2020. Motorized mode of transport (cars and two-wheelers) constitutes 85 percent of the total vehicles on roads. However, there are mismatches between transportation demand and supply in Indian cities. For example, in Delhi, 90 per cent of the road space is occupied by cars and two-wheelers, meeting not even 20 per cent of the travel demand. 
 The public transport system entails multiple benefits in terms of use of less road space, consumption of less fuel and less pollution per passenger. Yet, as per the NITI AAYOG 2018 report, India has only 1.2 buses per 1000 people which is far below the benchmarks for developing nations. Only 63 out of 458 Indian cities with populations exceeding 100,000 have a formal city bus system. Metro and suburban rail services operate in 13 and 9 cities, respectively. Inadequate public transport systems coupled with an increase in income levels have led to a rise in personalized transport. Intermediate public transport including auto rickshaws, taxis, and minibuses is bridging the transport supply gaps in the cities, especially the medium and smaller ones.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: Odd-even rule: DND flyway sees surge in early morning and late night traffic - The Economic Times
[METADATA/INTRO]: NOIDA: The DND Flyway has reported a surge in early morning and late night traffic after the odd-even trial began, indicating the reduced congestion on Delhi's roads doesn't necessarily mean a proportional decrease in the number of vehicles. What this...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: In contrast, the actual decrease in the number of vehicles crossing the DND after the odd-even trials began is around 4,000-5,000, officials said. This number is less than 5 per cent of the 1.2 lakh vehicles that traverse the DND every day. 
 The flyway has also seen a nearly 3 per cent rise in the number of two-wheelers crossing the toll plaza - from an average of 21,600 per day, the number of motorcycles and scooters has risen by around 700. Earlier, two-wheelers constituted 18-19 per cent of the total traffic flow. The percentage has now risen to 21-22 per cent, officials said. Two-wheelers are exempt from the odd-even rule. The trends indicate reaching office early during the 15-day trial period is being seen as a feasible alternative by people who drive to work regularly. The number of bikers has also increased at the Kalindi Kunj border and Noida Gate. "I have one car. So, I've started using my old bike on alternate days," said Mahesh Kumar, a commuter this correspondent spoke to on Tuesday at Kalindi Kunj.
